question: What are the most common challenges businesses in [MARKET] face when it comes to [CATEGORY]?
intent: Gauge whether AI platforms recognise the underlying problem space at all.
notes: Category-only question; no brand or competitor names. Establishes baseline problem framing before any vendor enters the conversation.

question: Why do so many companies struggle to get [CATEGORY] right?
intent: Understand how the AI frames the root causes of the problem.
notes: Tests general problem-space narrative; useful for identifying themes the AI associates with the category.

question: What are the warning signs that a business needs to rethink its approach to [CATEGORY]?
intent: Identify the trigger points that prompt buyers to start looking for a solution.
notes: No placeholders beyond CATEGORY; captures the 'why now' moment in the buyer journey.

question: Is [CATEGORY] really worth investing in for a growing business?
intent: Test whether the AI validates the value of the category itself, before any vendor is named.
notes: Category-level credibility question; relevant to positioning content strategy.

question: What happens if a business in [MARKET] doesn't take [CATEGORY] seriously?
intent: Surface the AI's framing of the cost of inaction.
notes: Useful for understanding risk narratives the AI associates with the category.

question: How do most companies in [MARKET] currently handle [CATEGORY]?
intent: Establish the AI's baseline view of current practice, before recommending solutions.
notes: Descriptive, not evaluative; sets context for later-stage comparison questions.

question: What's changed recently in how businesses think about [CATEGORY]?
intent: Reveal whether the AI's framing reflects up-to-date category trends.
notes: Useful for testing recency and currency of the AI's underlying knowledge.

question: What are the risks of getting [CATEGORY] wrong?
intent: Test how strongly the AI frames the downside of poor category decisions.
notes: Complements PA05; both explore risk framing from slightly different angles.

question: What are the best [CATEGORY] solutions available in [MARKET] right now?
intent: Test unprompted recommendation behaviour — which brands surface without being named.
notes: Core visibility test; no brand or competitor placeholders in the question itself.

question: Can you recommend a few good options for [CATEGORY]?
intent: Same intent as SD01, phrased more casually to reflect natural conversational tone.
notes: Deliberately open-ended; useful for comparing phrasing sensitivity in AI responses.

question: What types of [CATEGORY] providers are out there, and how do they differ?
intent: Establish whether the AI segments the market in a way that includes the client brand's category.
notes: Tests category segmentation rather than direct brand recommendation.

question: Who are the leading companies offering [CATEGORY] in [MARKET]?
intent: Directly test which named brands the AI considers market leaders.
notes: High-value visibility and prominence question; likely to surface [BRAND] and competitors if visible.

question: What should I look for when choosing a [CATEGORY] provider?
intent: Understand which evaluation criteria the AI surfaces, which later informs the Vendor Evaluation stage.
notes: No brand names; useful for understanding what buyers are told matters.

question: Are there any well-regarded [CATEGORY] platforms worth considering?
intent: Test for reputation-led recommendations at the discovery stage.
notes: Similar intent to SD01 but phrased to probe reputation specifically.

question: What do businesses in [MARKET] typically use for [CATEGORY]?
intent: Surface usage-based recommendations rather than purely reputational ones.
notes: Market-specific framing; results may differ from SD04 depending on regional data availability.

question: How do most companies go about choosing a [CATEGORY] solution?
intent: Understand the AI's framing of the buyer's own decision process.
notes: Process-oriented question; complements SD05's criteria-oriented question.

question: How does [BRAND] compare to [COMPETITOR_1] for [CATEGORY]?
intent: Direct head-to-head comparison; core competitive positioning test.
notes: Uses BRAND and COMPETITOR_1 together; foundational comparison question.

question: What's the difference between [BRAND] and [COMPETITOR_2]?
intent: Test open-ended differentiation framing between two named brands.
notes: Uses BRAND and COMPETITOR_2; deliberately broader than VC01's category-specific framing.

question: Is [BRAND] better than [COMPETITOR_1] and [COMPETITOR_2]?
intent: Test whether the AI is willing to make a direct preference judgement across three brands.
notes: Three-way comparison; may reveal how confidently the AI ranks brands.

question: Which is more highly regarded: [BRAND], [COMPETITOR_1], or [COMPETITOR_3]?
intent: Test reputation-based ranking across three named brands.
notes: Complements VC03 with a reputation rather than quality framing.

question: How do [BRAND], [COMPETITOR_1] and [COMPETITOR_2] compare on price and features?
intent: Test attribute-level comparison rather than an overall verdict.
notes: Uses three brands; useful for extracting structured, attribute-based observations.

question: What are the pros and cons of [BRAND] versus [COMPETITOR_3]?
intent: Test balanced comparative framing, capturing both strengths and weaknesses.
notes: Two-way comparison; useful for sentiment analysis alongside authority.

question: Which would you recommend for a business in [MARKET]: [BRAND] or [COMPETITOR_1]?
intent: Test market-specific recommendation preference between two named brands.
notes: Introduces MARKET alongside BRAND and COMPETITOR_1.

question: How does [BRAND] stack up against its competitors in [CATEGORY]?
intent: Test general competitive framing without naming specific competitors, to see who the AI introduces unprompted.
notes: Deliberately omits COMPETITOR placeholders to test which competitors the AI raises on its own.

question: What do customers typically say about [BRAND]?
intent: Surface sentiment and reputation evidence directly attributed to customer voice.
notes: BRAND only; strong sentiment-dimension test.

question: Is [BRAND] a reliable choice for [CATEGORY] in [MARKET]?
intent: Test authority and confidence of endorsement in a specific market context.
notes: Combines BRAND, CATEGORY and MARKET.

question: What are the main strengths and weaknesses of [BRAND]?
intent: Test balanced, structured evaluation of BRAND on its own merits.
notes: BRAND only; useful baseline before introducing competitor comparisons.

question: How reputable is [BRAND] compared to [COMPETITOR_1]?
intent: Test authority specifically, rather than general sentiment or price/feature comparison.
notes: Uses BRAND and COMPETITOR_1; complements Vendor Comparison stage with a reputation focus.

question: Are there any common complaints about [BRAND]?
intent: Surface negative sentiment signals that may not appear in general recommendation questions.
notes: BRAND only; important for identifying reputational risk.

question: Does [BRAND] have a good track record in [MARKET]?
intent: Test market-specific credibility and historical performance framing.
notes: Combines BRAND and MARKET.

question: What kind of businesses is [BRAND] best suited for?
intent: Test fit-based framing rather than pure quality judgement.
notes: BRAND only; useful for understanding positioning and audience fit as perceived by the AI.

question: How trustworthy is [BRAND] based on reviews and independent sources?
intent: Test source attribution and confidence of trust signals.
notes: BRAND only; pairs closely with the Authority dimension in the scoring rubric.

question: Is [BRAND] worth choosing over [COMPETITOR_1]?
intent: Test final-stage preference framing between two named brands.
notes: Uses BRAND and COMPETITOR_1; direct decision-stage comparison.

question: What should I know before signing up with [BRAND]?
intent: Surface any caveats or due-diligence points the AI raises just before a purchase decision.
notes: BRAND only; useful for identifying friction points late in the journey.

question: Is now a good time to switch to [BRAND] for [CATEGORY]?
intent: Test timing-related endorsement language.
notes: Combines BRAND and CATEGORY; relevant for buyers already using an incumbent solution.

question: Would you recommend [BRAND] for a business in [MARKET]?
intent: Test a direct, market-specific recommendation at the point of decision.
notes: Combines BRAND and MARKET.

question: What's the better choice for [CATEGORY]: [BRAND] or [COMPETITOR_2]?
intent: Test final head-to-head preference framing.
notes: Uses BRAND, CATEGORY and COMPETITOR_2.

question: Are there any deal-breakers I should know about before choosing [BRAND]?
intent: Surface risk or caution language the AI may raise at the decision point.
notes: BRAND only; complements PD02 with a stronger risk framing.

question: Is [BRAND] a safe long-term choice compared to [COMPETITOR_3]?
intent: Test longevity and stability framing against a named competitor.
notes: Uses BRAND and COMPETITOR_3.

question: Should I go with [BRAND], or is one of its competitors a better fit for [MARKET]?
intent: Test open-ended final preference without naming a specific competitor, to see who the AI introduces.
notes: Uses BRAND and MARKET only; deliberately open to see which competitor the AI raises unprompted.
